aWeek 3
1. Quick Recap 2. Few Facts 3. Orthogonality 4. Four Fundamental Subspaces 5. Few Proofs 6. Projection 7. Projection onto a line in R2R2 8. Projection onto a plane in R3R3 9. Projection onto a Subspace
1. Quick RecapEuclidean Distance between Two VectorsLet u,v∈Rdu,v∈Rd. The euclidean distance between uu and vv is given by ||u-v||||u-v|| or ||v-u||||v-u||. Linear CombinationLet VV be a vector space. Let v1,v2,...,vn∈Vv1,v2,...,vn∈V and let a1,a2,...,an∈Ra1,a2,...,an∈R.z=a1v1+a2v2+...+anvn=n∑i=1ai·viz=a1v1+a2v2+...+anvn=∑i=1nai·vizz is a linear combination of v1,v2,...,vn∈Vv1,v2,...,vn∈V. Span SetLet S={v1,v2,...,vn}S={v1,v2,...,vn} be a set of vectors in RdRd where d∈Rd∈R. The span set of SS is defined as follows.
span(S)={n∑i=1ai·viavi∈S and ai∈R}
span(S)={∑i=1nai·vi|vi∈Sandai∈R}In simple words, span(S)span(S) is the set of all possible linear combinations of v1,v2,...,vnv1,v2,...,vn. NOTE : span(S)span(S) is a vector space. Linear independence and Linear dependence.Let S={v1,v2,...,vn}S={v1,v2,...,vn} be a set of vectors in RdRd where d∈Rd∈R. Consider the following systema

[|||v1v2⋯vn|||]×[x1x2⋮xn]=[0⋮0] • If this system has a unique solution, then SS is a linearly independent set.• If this system has infinitely many solutions, the SS is a linearly dependent set. In simple words, if there exists only one way to linearly combine the vectors in set SS to get a
00⋯0
T[00⋯0]T, then SS is linearly independent. If there are more than one way to linearly combine the vectors in set SS to get a
00⋯0
T[00⋯0]T, then SS is linearly dependent. NOTE : If set SS is linearly independent, then a
x1x2⋯xn
T=a
00⋯0
T[x1x2⋯xn]T=[00⋯0]T. This is beacuse, a
00⋯0
T[00⋯0]T is always a solution to a homogeneous system of linear equations. Basis and Dimension of a Vector SpaceLet VV be a vector space. Let BB be a subset of VV. BB is a basis of VV if it satisfies the following conditions.• BB is a linearly independent set• span(B)=Vspan(B)=V If BB is a basis of VV, then the dimension of VV is the cardinality of BB. In simple words, the dimension of VV is the number of elements in the basis of VV. The dimension of a vector space is unique. The basis set is not unique. Rank of a MatrixLet AA be an m×nm×n matrix.
The rank of AA is the number of linearly independent columns of AA
 • Column space of AA →→ span{All the column vectors of A}span{AllthecolumnvectorsofA}• Column rank of AA →→ dim(Column space of A)dim(ColumnspaceofA). • Row space of AA →→ span{All the row vecors of A}span{AlltherowvecorsofA}• Row rank of AA →→ dim(Row space of A)dim(RowspaceofA) Nullity of a MatrixLet AA be an m×nm×n matrix. The set of all the solutions for Ax=0Ax=0 is the nullspace of AA.Nullspace(A)={x|Ax=0}Nullspace(A)={x|Ax=0} The dimension of the Nullspace(A)Nullspace(A) is the nullity of AA.
2. Few FactsLet AA be a matrix. There are two facts mentioned below, that are related AA. Though the proofs for these two are not given, both are accompanied by small hints which are useful to prove these two facts. Readers are requested to revisit the concepts Maths-2 for a complete proof.Row Rank(A) = Column Rank(A)RowRank(A)=ColumnRank(A)HINT Let RR be the reduced row echelon form of AA. • The row rank of AA is the number of non-zero rows in R. • The column rank of AA is the number of pivot columns in RR.The number of non-zero rows in RR and the number of pivot columns in RR are equal. Rank(A)+Nullity(A)=No. of columns in ARank(A)+Nullity(A)=No.ofcolumnsinAHINTFor the system of linear equations denoted by Ax=0Ax=0, let (A|0)(A|0) be the augmented matrix. Let (R|0)(R|0) be the reduced row echelon form of (A|0)(A|0). • The number of dependent variables is equal to the rank of AA• The number of independent variables is equal to the nullity of AA.The sum of the number of independent variables and the number of dependent variables is equal to the number of columns in AA.
3. OrthogonalityTwo vectors u,v∈Rdu,v∈Rd are othogonal to each other if u·v=0u·v=0. If the dot product of two vectors is 00, then the angle between them is 90°90°. Orthogonal SetA set V∈RdV∈Rd is an orthogonal set, if every pair of distinct vectors in VV is orthogonal. Theorem : An orthogonal set ((without the zero vector)) is a linearly independent setProof: Let V={v1,v2,...,vn}V={v1,v2,...,vn} be an orthogonal set. Let ci∈Rci∈R, i∈{1,...,n}i∈{1,...,n}
c1v1+c2v2+....+cnvn=0
v1·[c1v1+c2v2+...+cnvn]=0·v1
c1(v1·v1)+c2(v1·v2)+...+cn(v1·vn)=0
c1×||v1||2=0
c1=0
c1v1+c2v2+....+cnvn=0v1·[c1v1+c2v2+...+cnvn]=0·v1c1(v1·v1)+c2(v1·v2)+...+cn(v1·vn)=0c1×||v1||2=0c1=0Similarly, we can show that ci=0, ∀i∈{1,...,n}ci=0,∀i∈{1,...,n}. Therefore, VV is a linearly independent set. Orthonormal SetA set V∈RdV∈Rd is an orthonormal set, if the following conditions are true:• VV is an orthogonal set• ||u||=1, ∀u∈V||u||=1,∀u∈V Orthogonal SubspaceTwo subspaces U, V∈RdU,V∈Rd are orthogonal subspaces if every vector in UU is orthogonal to every vector in VV and vice-versa. In other words, if UU and VV are orthogonal subspaces, then, u·v=0, ∀u∈U & ∀v∈Vu·v=0,∀u∈U&∀v∈V Examples:• The XY-planeXY-plane and the ZZ axis are orthogonal subspaces in R3R3.• The X-axisX-axis and the Y-axisY-axis are orthogonal subspace in R2R2 Orthogonal ComplementsLet VV be a vector space. Two orthogonal subspaces of VV, given by M & NM&N are orthogonal complements, if any one of the conditions given below hold true: • dimaMa+dimaNa=dimaVadim(M)+dim(N)=dim(V)• V=spanaBasis(M)∪Basis(N)aV=span(Basis(M)∪Basis(N))• N=ax∈Vam·x=0, ∀m∈MaN={x∈V|m·x=0,∀m∈M}• M=ax∈Van·x=0, ∀n∈NaM={x∈V|n·x=0,∀n∈N} Infact, these four conditions are equivalent. Examples:• The XY-planeXY-plane and the ZZ axis are orthogonal complements in R3R3.• The X-axisX-axis and the Y-axisY-axis are orthogonal complements in R2R2

Row Space of AAThe row space of AA, denoted by R(A)R(A), is given by the following set. R(A)=span({r1,r2,...,rm})R(A)=span({r1,r2,...,rm}) ➔ R(A)⊆RnR(A)⊆Rn➔ dimaR(A)adim(R(A)) is called the row rank of AA Column Space of AAThe column space of AA, denoted by C(A)C(A), is given by the following set. C(A)=span({c1,c2,...,cn})C(A)=span({c1,c2,...,cn})➔ C(A)⊆RmC(A)⊆Rm➔ dimaC(A)adim(C(A)) is called the column rank of AA Null Space of AAThe null space of AA, denoted by N(A)N(A), is given by the following set. N(A)=ax∈RnaAx=0aN(A)={x∈Rn|Ax=0} ➔ N(A)⊆RnN(A)⊆Rn➔ dimaN(A)adim(N(A)) is called the nullity of AA If a vector v∈N(A)v∈N(A), then ri·v=0, ∀i∈{1,2,...,m}ri·v=0,∀i∈{1,2,...,m}. That is, every vector in the null space of AA is orthogonal to every row vector of AA, and, vice-versa. Ax=0 ↓ a

Null Space of ATAT The null space of ATAT, also know as the left null space of AA, denoted by N(AT)N(AT), is given by the following set. N(AT)=ax∈RmaATx=0aN(AT)={x∈Rm|ATx=0} ➔ N(AT)⊆RmN(AT)⊆Rm➔ dimaN(AT)adim(N(AT)) is called the nullity of ATAT If a vector v∈N(AT)v∈N(AT), then cj·v=0, ∀j∈{1,2,...,n}cj·v=0,∀j∈{1,2,...,n}. That is, every vector in the left null space of AA is orthogonal to every column vector of AA, and, vice-versa. ATx=0 ↓ a

5. Few ProofsBefore we look at projections, lets look at a few theorems and their proofs. In this section, II is an identity matrix. TheoremThe inverse of an invertible matrix is unique. Proof:Let AA be an invertible matrix. Let BB & CC be matrices such that: AB=BA=IAB=BA=I and AC=CA=IAC=CA=I.
B=B
=BI
=BAC
=IC
=C
B=B=BI=BAC=IC=C Since B=CB=C, the inverse of an invertible matrix is unique. TheoremIf SS is an invertible and symmetric matrix, i.e., ST=SST=S, then S-1S-1 is also symmetric. Proof:
S-1S=I
(S-1S)T=IT
ST(S-1)T=I
S(S-1)T=I
S-1S(S-1)T=S-1I
I(S-1)T=S-1
(S-1)T=S-1
S-1S=I(S-1S)T=ITST(S-1)T=IS(S-1)T=IS-1S(S-1)T=S-1II(S-1)T=S-1(S-1)T=S-1TheoremThe nullspace of ATAATA and AA are the same. Proof:To prove this, we have to show that every element in N(A)N(A) is also an element in N(ATA)N(ATA) and every element in N(ATA)N(ATA) is also an element in N(A)N(A). Let v∈N(A)v∈N(A). Then,
Av=0

ATAu=0uTATAu=0(Au)TAu=0||Au||2=0Au=0Therefore, u∈N(A)u∈N(A).
6. ProjectionConsider a vector space V∈RdV∈Rd and a vector b∈Rdb∈Rd. The goal of projection is to find a vector in VV that is closest to bb. Among all the vectors in VV, the vector which is closest to bb is called the projecton of bb onto VV. We shall first visualize projection onto a line in R2R2 and projection onto a plane in R3R3 and then generalize the idea of projection.
7. Projection onto a line in R2R2Let b,w∈R2b,w∈R2. A line LL is given as follows: L=span({w})L=span({w})Clearly, LL is a subspace in R2R2. GOAL : Find the projection of bb onto LL.
LLbbppeewwLet us refer to the projection of bb onto LL as pp. Since p∈Lp∈L, pp will be a scalar multiple of ww. We can write p=cwp=cw, where c∈Rc∈R Among all the vectors in LL, pp is the vector that is closest to bb. The shortest distance is also the perpendicular distance. Therefore, the vector e=b-pe=b-p is perpendicular to the line LL. The figure shows that e+p=be+p=b. This is because of the parallelogram law of addition. The vector ee is called the error vector. The length of this vector gives the distance between bb and pp. Since ee is perpendicular to the line LL, we can write a
w·e=0
w·(b-p)=0
w·(b-cw)=0
w·b-c||w||2=0
c=w·b
||w||2
w·e=0w·(b-p)=0w·(b-cw)=0w·b-c||w||2=0c=w·b||w||2 Once we find cc, find pp is simple, as p=cwp=cw.
8. Projection onto a plane in R3R3Let w1,w2,b∈R3w1,w2,b∈R3. Also, let w1w1 and w2w2 be linearly independent.A plane ZZ in R3R3 is given as follows: Z=span({w1,w2})Z=span({w1,w2}) Clearly, ZZ is a subspace of R3R3. GOAL : Find the projection of bb onto LL
w1w1w2w2bbppeeZZ Let us refer to the projection of bb onto ZZ as pp. Since p∈Zp∈Z, pp will be a ZZ will be a linear combination of w1w1 and w2w2. We can write p=𝛼w1+𝛽w2p=𝛼w1+𝛽w2, where 𝛼,𝛽∈R𝛼,𝛽∈R. Among all the vectors in ZZ, pp is the closest to bb. Again, the shortest distance is the perpendicular distance. Therefore, e=b-pe=b-p is perpendicular to the plane ZZ. Once again, the picture abve shows that the parallelogram law of addition holds true for e+p=be+p=b. Here too, ee is called the error vector, whose length captures the distance between bb and pp. Since ee is perpendicular to the plane ZZ we can write,
w1·e=0
w1·(b-p)=0
w1·(b-𝛼w1-𝛽w2)=0
w1·b-𝛼(w1·w1)-𝛽(w1·w2)=0
𝛼(w1·w1)+𝛽(w1·w2)=w1·b
w1·e=0w1·(b-p)=0w1·(b-𝛼w1-𝛽w2)=0w1·b-𝛼(w1·w1)-𝛽(w1·w2)=0𝛼(w1·w1)+𝛽(w1·w2)=w1·b
w2·e=0
w2·(b-p)=0
w2·(b-𝛼w1-𝛽w2)=0
w2·b-𝛼(w2·w1)-𝛽(w2·w2)=0
𝛼(w2·w1)+𝛽(w2·w2)=w2·b
w2·e=0w2·(b-p)=0w2·(b-𝛼w1-𝛽w2)=0w2·b-𝛼(w2·w1)-𝛽(w2·w2)=0𝛼(w2·w1)+𝛽(w2·w2)=w2·b The matrix for of linear equations (1)(1) and (2)(2) is Px=qPx=q, where P=a

A=[||w1w2||]. The columns of AA are linearly independent. As a result, N(A)={0}N(A)={0}. This means, N(ATA)={0}N(ATA)={0}. Therefore, ATAATA is invertible. Px=qPx=q will have a unique solution.

9. Projection onto a SubspaceLet V⊆RdV⊆Rd be a subspace with dim(V)=ndim(V)=n. The following is a basis set for VV: {v1,v2,...,vn}{v1,v2,...,vn}Let b∈Rdb∈Rd. GOAL : Project bb onto VV. Let us define a matrix AA as followsA=a
|| |
v1v2⋯vn
|| |
A=[|||v1v2⋯vn|||] The columns of AA are linearly independent. Let pp be the projection of bb onto VV. Since p∈Vp∈V, we can write p=Axp=Ax, where x=a

ATe=0AT(b-p)=0AT(b-Ax)=0ATb-ATAx=0ATAx=ATbx=(ATA)-1ATb Since the columns of AA are linearly independent, N(A)={0}N(A)={0}. This means, N(ATA)={0}N(ATA)={0}. Therefore, ATAATA is invertible. The projection pp is given by:
p=Ax

p=Ax=A(ATA)-1ATb=Pb Here, P=A(ATA)-1ATP=A(ATA)-1AT is referred to as the projection matrix. Properties of Projection MatrixProperty-1 : PP is a symmetric matrix. That is, PT=PPT=P.Proof:
PT=(A(ATA)-1AT)T
=(AT)T((ATA)-1)TAT
=A(ATA)-1AT
PT=(A(ATA)-1AT)T=(AT)T((ATA)-1)TAT=A(ATA)-1ATFor the above proof, we have made use of the following:• (ABC)T=CTBTAT(ABC)T=CTBTAT• ATAATA is symmetric, therefore, (ATA)-1(ATA)-1 is also symmetric. That is, ((ATA)-1)T=(ATA)-1((ATA)-1)T=(ATA)-1. Property-2 : P2=PP2=PProof:
P2=P×P
=(A(ATA)-1AT)(A(ATA)-1AT)
=A(ATA)-1(ATA)(ATA)-1AT
=A(ATA)-1AT
P2=P×P=(A(ATA)-1AT)(A(ATA)-1AT)=A(ATA)-1(ATA)(ATA)-1AT=A(ATA)-1ATFor the above proof, we have made use of the following:• (AB)C=A(BC)(AB)C=A(BC)• (ATA)-1(ATA)=I(ATA)-1(ATA)=I, where II is the identity matrix. What does it mean to say that P2=PP2=P ??Suppose we are projecting a vector bb onto VV. The projection is PbPb. What happens if we project PbPb onto VV? Notice that Pb∈VPb∈V. Projecting a vector onto a subspace in which it is already a part, results in the same vector. The projection of PbPb onto VV is given by P×Pb=P2b=PbP×Pb=P2b=Pb.
